Notice Of Motion And Motion To Reopen Discovery And For Leave To Conduct Expert Witness Exchange
Off Calendar Dec-10-2025 Continued To Dec-30-25 At 09:00 A.M. In Dept 302
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF PAUL HENRY's Motion To Reopen Discovery And For Leave To Conduct Expert Witness Exchange.

Code of Civil Procedure 2024.050(a) requires a party to meet and confer with his or her opponents prior to filing a motion to re-open discovery. Here, Defendants Smith, Young, Kampouris, Keating and Mimura contend that Plaintiff's efforts to meet and confer were inadequate. Plaintiff responds that when he raised the issue of re-opening discovery during email exchanges regarding continuing the trial date in August and September 2025, Defendants were adamantly opposed. Some evidence supports Plaintiff's assumption that further efforts to meet and confer prior to filing the motion might be futile.

The court nevertheless believes that requiring the parties to participate in a good faith effort to resolve this issue would promote judicial economy and possibly give raise to a negotiated resolution, Accordingly, the court directs the parties to meet and confer via telephone and/or email on the issue of whether discovery, including expert witness disclosure, should be re-opened prior to the May 11, 2026 trial date. Plaintiff's motion shall be continued for hearing to December 30, 2025 at 9:00 a.m. Each side shall file and serve a declaration documenting the results of the court-ordered meet and confer on or before December 23, 2025.

A proposed order is submitted for the court's signature.

Visiting Judge: Cindee Mayfield, Clerk: M. Goodman, Not Reported. (302/CM)

Notice Of Motion, Motion For Order Establishing Admissions And For Sanctions, Memorandum In Support Thereof, And Declaration Of Kelly M. Peterson
Off Calendar Jul-17-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

1 - DEFENDANT EMMANUEL KAMPOURIS' Motion For Order Establishing Admissions And For Sanctions.

Defendant Emmanuel Kampouris's motion for order establishing admissions is DENIED. Defendant's request for sanctions is GRANTED.

Under CCP 2033.280(c), the court shall deem requests for admissions (RFAs) admitted, unless it finds the responding party, before hearing on the motion, served a proposed response to the requests for admission that is in substantial compliance with CCP 2033.220. Here, Plaintiff served substantially compliant, verified responses without objections on June 18, 2025. (Aalaei decl. Ex. E.) Thus, the request for an order establishing admissions is denied.

As for Defendant's sanctions request, the court must impose monetary sanctions on the party or attorney whose failure to serve a timely responses necessitates the motion. (CCP 2033.280(c).) Here, defense counsel served RFAs to plaintiff's counsel Aalaei via email on May 5, 2025. Accounting for e-service, the deadline to respond fell on June 6, 2025. (CCP 2033.250; CCP 1010.6.) Defendant's failure to copy co-counsel on this email did not extend the deadline, as service upon Aalaei sufficed. Plaintiff belatedly served his original responses consisting of objections on June 16, 2025. By then, his objections were waived under CCP 2033.280, necessitating this motion. The court orders Plaintiff and his attorney Ali Aalaei, jointly and severally, to pay Kampouris $1,087.50 in sanctions by August 14, 2025.

Order is signed.

Judge: Joseph M. Quinn, Clerk: M. Goodman, Not Reported. (302/JMQ)

Defendant served his second set of form interrogatories on May 5, 2025. Accounting for e-service, the deadline to respond fell on June 6, 2025. (CCP 2030.260; CCP 1010.6.) Plaintiff failed to timely provide any responses. Plaintiff belatedly served his original responses consisting of objections on June 16, 2025. By then, his objections were waived under CCP 2030.290. Plaintiff's waived objections did not amount to any response, and thus no meet and confer was required. (Leach v. Superior Court (1980) 111 Cal.App.3d 902, 905.) Where no responses are received, no meet and confer is required and no separate statement is required.

Plaintiff must provide Code-compliant, objection free responses to Kampouris's form interrogatories, set two by July 30, 2025.

Plaintiff lacked substantial justification in opposing this motion. The court orders Plaintiff and his counsel, jointly and severally, to pay Kampouris $1,087.50 in sanctions by August 14, 2025.

Order is signed.

Judge: Joseph M. Quinn, Clerk: M. Goodman, Not Reported. (302/JMQ)

Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order Establishing Admissions And For Sanctions
Off Calendar Jul-11-2025 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT ANDREW LEWIS' Motion For Order Establishing Admissions And For Sanctions.

Defendant Andrew Lewis' motion for an order establishing admissions and for monetary sanctions is denied in its entirety. Plaintiff Paul Henry's request for monetary sanctions is also denied. This is yet another motion in this case that should not have been filed. The conduct of the parties' counsel on both sides does not comport with what is expected of thoughtful attorneys who are desirous of resolving their clients' disputes in a civil, professional and cost-effective manner. Because both sides acted without substantial justification, no monetary sanctions are awarded.

Although Mr. Lewis surely knew, or at least should have known, that all or nearly all of his requests for admission would be denied, the requests still had some arguable utility and needed to be responded to seriously. Mr. Henry's initial objection-only responses were frivolous and not in good faith. Mr. Lewis' assertion that the responses were due on June 10 lacks merit since it would require that the 2 days additional days for electronic service run from Sunday June 8, which is contrary to CCP 10 (every Sunday is a holiday) and 12a ("If the last day for the performance of any act ... is a holiday, then that period is hereby extended to ... the next day that is not a holiday). Thus, the premise of this motion that the objection-only responses were untimely is in error. But even if not in error, Mr. Lewis should not have filed the motion the day after the deadline for the responses without any meet and confer effort. Mr. Henry's first supplemental responses consisting of denials of every request without any objections show that Mr. Henry could and should have made such responses as his initial responses, which, had he done so, might have prevented the filing of this motion. Mr. Lewis' refusal to withdraw this motion by Mr. Smith is also troubling, as is both sides' inflated request for fees, especially the $8,000 sought by Mr. Henry based on a claimed 18 hours of work for opposition papers that should have taken significantly less than half that time.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling in complince with CRC 3.1312.

Off Calendar Jun-17-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Plaintiff Paul Henry's motion to compel a further response from defendant Eric Young to special interrogatory number four and for monetary sanctions is granted in large part.

No later than July 1, 2025 Mr. Young: 1) must provide a verified further response to special interrogatory number 4 stating, without any objections and after a diligent search for and review of the information in his possession and from sources that are available to him, the name of the bank, the address of the principal place of business of the bank or the branch(es) he has used for that bank and the account number of each bank account owned in whole or in part by himself, Cerego, Inc., Cerego Japan Kabushiki Kaishand Memre, Inc. from January 1, 2021 to the present and 2) pay $2,800 in monetary sanctions to Mr. Henry.

While special interrogatory number 4 could have been worded better, there is no uncertainty as to the information sought by that interrogatory, especially when the interrogatory is reviewed in light of Mr. Henry's complaint. All of the objections asserted by Mr. Young lack merit and the meager information provided by him is insufficient. All of the objections are stricken. The information sought by the interrogatory is directly relevant to Mr. Henry's claims and thus any financial privacy rights of Mr. Young or others is not an impediment to providing a full response to the interrogatory. Nor is CCP 3295(c) implicated by the interrogatory. Although Mr. Henry could have requested the information he seeks by four separate interrogatories, His failure to do so did not adversely impact Mr. Young's ability to respond. The omission of a portion of Mr. Young's further responses from Mr. Henry's separate statement did not prejudice Mr. Young. Mr. Young's failure to state that he conducted a diligent search for and review of the information in his possession and from sources that available to him renders his responses insufficient.

Mr. Young's responses are evasive and thus lack substantial justification. Reasonable monetary sanctions are calculated at 7 hours of attorney time at a blended hourly rate of $400.

Off Calendar May-28-2025 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances (Via Zoom videoconference).
Ali Ari Aalaei, Esq. of ARI LAW, P.C., Tel: (415) 830-9968 for Plaintiff.

Kelly M. Peterson, ESQ. of THOMPSON HINE LLP. Tel: 310.998.9100 for defendant Emmanuel Kampouris.
Court Reporter: Nancy Presant, CSR# 9906, Tel:510.757.8550, email: NpresantCSR@gmail.com.

Matter not argued/contested. The court adopts its tentative ruling.

PLAINTIFF PAUL HENRY's Motion To Strike Defendant Emmanuel Kampouris'S Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Of Defendant Andrew Smith Lewis'S Motion For Sanctions Under Ccp Section 128.7 is DENIED.

Plaintiff movers under Code of Civil Procedure section 435. "Any party, within the time allowed to respond to a pleading may serve and file a notice of motion to strike the whole or any part thereof..." (CCP 435.) "The court may, upon a motion made pursuant to Section 435, or at any time in its discretion, and upon terms it deems proper: [para] (a) Strike out any irrelevant, false, or improper matter inserted in any pleading. (b) Strike out all or any part of any pleading not drawn or filed in conformity with the laws of this state, a court rule, or an order of the court." (CCP 436.)

Here, Plaintiff has failed to demonstrate Defendant Kampouris's memorandum is a "pleading." Nor had Plaintiff demonstrated the memorandum is prohibited under Code of Civil Procedure section 128.7. Additionally, the motion that the memorandum supported has been decided. Even if there were a basis to strike the memorandum, the court would not exercise its discretion under section 435 in favor of striking it.

Order Signed.

Off Calendar Feb-18-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances (by Zoom):

KENNETH R. VAN VLECK, Esq. GCA LAW PARTNERS LLP (650) 428-3900 for Defendant ERic Young.
Kelly Peterson, Esq. 310-998-9100 Thompson Hine for Defendant Emmanuel Kampouris.

ARGUED. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF PAUL HENRY's Hearing For Right To Attach Order For Issuance Of Writ Of Attachment.

Plaintiff Paul Henry's application for a right to attach order and writ of attachment is denied. Mr. Henry's application is not based on a contract claim. Mr. Henry does not allege that he has a contract with either defendant Eric Young or defendant Memre.AI. Nor are any of the claims in his complaint based on breach of a contract. His first cause of action is for fraud, a tort claim. His second through fifth causes of action are statutory claims. Recognizing that he has not alleged a contract claim in this case, Mr. Henry argues that his claims are "fundamentally contractual" and "rooted" in contractual obligations. But California law requires a contract claim to support an attachment, not a non-contract claim that is fundamentally contractual or rooted in contractual obligations. Mr. Henry does not cite any authority permitting an attachment for a non-contract claim that is fundamentally contractual or rooted in contractual obligations, nor is the court aware of any such authority.

Order is signed.

MOTION FOR JUDGMENT ON THE PLEADINGS
Off Calendar Dec-10-2024 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

CROSS DEFENDANT PAUL HENRY's MOTION FOR JUDGMENT ON THE PLEADINGS.

Paul Henry's motion for judgment on the pleadings as to Eric Young's first amended cross-complaint for declaratory and injunctive relief (FACC) is denied. Young pleads that he owns intellectual property obtained from Cerego Japan as his employment compensation and that Henry seeks assignment of this same IP to help satisfy a judgment he has against a former employer, Cerego, Inc. (FACC 1:27-2:8, 4:27-5:4.) Thus, this is a classic scenario for CCP 1060 declaratory relief. Covering territory that was (or should have been) covered by an earlier unsuccessful demurrer, Henry's motion makes two unavailing arguments. First, he says he and Young must have a "relationship." (Memo. 6:4-5.) They do: both want the same IP. But CCP 1060 is not so restricted in any event. Second, Henry says the FACC seeks an "advisory opinion." (Memo. 6:12.) But what it actually seeks is not conjectural: "a judicial determination of the respective ownership, rights and duties" of Young and Henry "with respect to the intellectual property rights." (FACC 5:5-10.)

Proposed order submitted.

DEMURRER to Amended CROSS COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Oct-23-2024 Overruled
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. KENNETH R. VAN VLECK, Esq. of GCA Law Partners LLP (Tel: 650-428-3900) for cross-complainant Eric Young;
Atty. ALI A. AALAEI, Esq. of Ari Law, P.C. (Tel: 415-830-9968) cross-defendant Paul Henry;

HEARING RE: CROSS-DEFENDANT PAUL HENRY'S DEMURRER TO FIRST AMENDED CROSS-COMPLAINT.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Cross-defendant Paul Henry's ("Henry") demurrer to the amended cross-complaint is overruled.

The demurrer based on a defect or misjoinder of parties is overruled. (CCP 430.10(d).) The amended cross-complaint seeks to resolve the intellectual property rights between Henry and Eric Young ("Young"). Cerego Japan is not a necessary or indispensable party since both parties agree that it no longer owns the intellectual property rights. Either Young or Cerago Inc. now own the property rights. Henry also fails to show how the failure to join Cerego Japan prejudices him. (See Anaya v. Superior Court (1984) 160 Cal.App.3d 228, 231 fn. 1 [defendant arguing misjoinder entitled to favorable ruling only when it can show prejudice and a proper defendant is seldom prejudiced by improper joinder].)

The demurrer based on failure to state a cause of action is overruled. (CCP 430.10(e).) The amended cross-complaint pleads an actual controversy between the parties regarding the ownership of the intellectual property rights. Henry fails to show that claim or issue preclusion applies. Young was not a party to the fraudulent transfer action (CGC-22-600130) nor has Henry shown that Young was a privy of Cerego Japan. In addition, Young's rights were never actually litigated since that action ended by default judgment. (See DKN Holdings LLC v Faerber (2015) 61 Cal.4th 813, 824 ["Claim preclusion 'prevents relitigation of the same cause of action in a second suit between the same parties or parties in privity with them.'"]; Id. at 825 [issue preclusion applied to issues "actually litigated"].)

The demurrer for uncertainty is overruled. (CCP 430.10(f).) Demurrers for uncertainty are disfavored and Henry can reasonably respond to the pleading. (Chen v. Berenjian (2019) 33 Cal.App.5th 811, 822.)

The demurrer based on the failure to plead the type of contract is overruled. (CCP 430.10(g).) Young is not alleging a breach of contract claim.

The court did not entertain the new issues that Henry raises in the reply (advisory opinion, privity). Those issues should have been briefed in Henry's threadbare moving papers.

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: S. Kane; Not Reported. (302/RBU)
